elements.sent-2411-004: This paper explores a novel three-year dataset of survey responses from thousands of people experiencing homelessness collected via network-based sampling (peer referral methods), employed to estimate the total number of unsheltered people in King County, WA (including the Seattle metro area), from 2022 to 2024.

elements.sent-2411-006: We analyze the mean degree, degree distribution, and mixing rates of King County's unsheltered population, along with four core network measures: an acquaintance network, a close friendship network, a kinship network, and a peer referral network.

elements.sent-2411-016: The social networks of people experiencing homelessness are an understudied and important aspect of the lives of unhoused people in the United States (US).
elements.sent-2411-017: The personal networks of social and economic support \citep{small2013weak,small2017someone play an important role in policy, support, and interventions (e.g., improved sleep locations or housing) for this population \citep{joly2014supporting,kennedy2022restructuring .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Personal networks for the unhoused population provide access to information (e.g., shelters, new programs, resources), amenities (e.g., access to phones, food or shelter), emotional support (e.g., someone to talk to), and safety (e.g., physical safety).
elements.sent-2411-019: These networks nudge decisions about where people spend their time, where they work, who they go to for help, and ultimately whether they stay in housing once they obtain it \citep{vsimon2019activity .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Social support is known to help people move out of substance use situations and help them not use in the first place \citep{rapier2019inverse .}
elements.sent-2411-021: There are, however, only few studies on the networks between people experiencing homelessness \cite[e.g.,][]{anderson2021ecology,anderson2023norms,almquist2020large,green2013social , and qualitative and quantitative studies on the importance of social support networks for the unhoused population \citep{cummings2022social,solarz1990social,green2013social,groton2019social .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Here we seek to follow up this literature with a population level sample of people experiencing homelessness in King County, WA\footnote{The twelfth largest county by population in the US with 2.3 Million people and the third largest population of unhoused people, with more than 16,000 people living outdoors or in temporary shelters on any given night.}
elements.sent-2411-023: People living unhoused are typically considered a hard-to-reach population \citep{almquist2024innovating,killworth1998estimation , but it is a growing and socially important community across the US.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The US Department of Housing and Urban Development's (HUD) Annual Homeless Assessment Report (AHAR) -- presented to the US Congress in December 2023 -- reported 653,100 people experiencing homelessness in the United States on any given night.
elements.sent-2411-025: This number is estimated from two key measurements: (1) the sheltered counts from the Homeless Management Information System (HMIS) databases and allied service provider records contained in the HUD-designated administrative/geographic unit known as continuum-of-care \cite[see for details][]{almquist2020connecting , and (2) the unsheltered count \citep{richards2023unsheltered , which comes from the Point-in-Time (PIT) count conducted biennially every January within a given federally defined local jurisdiction\footnote{These jurisdictions are known as Continuum of Care (COC) by HUD.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Traditionally, this PIT count is conducted on a single night in January by volunteers and is performed as a visual census of people sleeping in the streets \citep{almquist2020connecting .}
elements.sent-2411-027: For example, in King County, WA, 2020, this was conducted from 2:00 AM to 6:00 AM with around 1,000 volunteers, followed in the next few weeks by a demographic survey in day centers and emergency shelters \citep{home2020seattle .}
elements.sent-2411-028: This process has been scrutinized recently \cite[see,][]{tsai2022annual , and new methods have been proposed in recent years.}
elements.sent-2411-029: This method was implemented using network sampling -- specifically respondent-driven sampling (RDS) \citep{heckathorn1997respondent,gile2010respondent in 2022 and again in 2024 with large-scale unofficial county-wide RDS in 2023.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Over the last three years, in King County, WA, this network-based approach has produced the most extensive set of network data that we know of on the US unhoused population.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this paper, we will analyze the resulting network data to understand the social networks of the unhoused population in King County, WA, with an eye on how this can be used to improve social policy and outcomes of the unhoused community.
elements.sent-2411-032: The data collected in this study included several aggregate personal network measures: (1) the total number of people experiencing homelessness who ego\footnote{The focal node in a network, in this case, the survey respondent \citet{almquist2012random,wasserman1994social .}}

elements.sent-2411-034: Our goal in this paper is to understand the change in the social structure of people experiencing homelessness from 2022 to 2024 and to describe the networks of the unhoused community in a large urban, suburban, and rural area.
elements.sent-2411-035: We will be looking specifically at the differences and similarities of these four networks across demographics and other characteristics with an eye to how these networks can inform policy (e.g., improve information flow during a natural hazard).
elements.sent-2411-036: We explore these four networks over the three waves (2022, 2023, and 2024) of data collected in \citet{almquist2024innovating to understand the basic network/relational structure of people experiencing homelessness in King County.}

elements.sent-2411-038: Overall, our samples consist of three years of cross-sectional data collected through peer referral methods.
elements.sent-2411-039: Specifically, this process followed network recruitment of respondents (ego in network parlance) through ego's acquaintanceship of people experiencing homelessness (alters in network language).
elements.sent-2411-040: Starting in 2022, we surveyed 671 people experiencing homelessness; in 2023, 1,106; and in 2024, 1,466, using network methods to estimate the unsheltered population in King County, WA.
elements.sent-2411-041: The paper is laid out as follows: we first provide basic information on the study design and participants, then we explore the networks of people experiencing homelessness through first careful analysis of their personal network \cite[e.g., degree or count of individuals connected to the focal person (or node);][]{wasserman1994social over the three core survey measures; we follow this up with statistical models of the personal network size by demographic and social attributes.}

elements.sent-2411-043: Last, we discuss the implications of these results, including simulating complete networks by fitting an exponential-family random graph model to the observed network statistics, and we conclude by discussing the results and implications for future research.

elements.sent-2411-126: One of the defining characteristics of an RDS study is that it is a diffusion process, where we can think of this as a basic measure of how information or goods would flow through the community of people experiencing homelessness.
elements.sent-2411-127: We can categorize the typology of this diffusion process as expressed in the isomorphism \citep{wasserman1994social of the recruitment trees (See Figure~\ref{fig:rdstree for the 2023 and 2024 tree isomorphism observed).}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Overall, we see many small diffusion chains with a handful of cases that take off \cite[similar to another diffusion process that has been studied, such as those of diffusion processes over online networks such as Twitter or Facebook]{goel2012structure .}
elements.sent-2411-129: The implication of these diffusion chain results suggests that in the case of getting out emergency information -- e.g., the heat warnings like the heat dome in Seattle, WA in 2021, where the temperatures reached 107 degrees Fahrenheit or 42 degrees Celsius, and information on where cooling stations would be available was utmost importance -- depends on finding a few well-connected people which can result in large and fast cascades of information flow through the community.
elements.sent-2411-130: This backs up the simulation results in \cite{almquist2020large .}

elements.section-2411-004: Count data model for degree distribution in the four networks
elements.sent-2411-134: In this section, we consider just the 2024 networks, where we model them as zero-inflated negative binomial models, as discussed in the Methods section.
elements.sent-2411-135: The results for 2023 can be found in \ref{countdatamodel2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-136: The four models see a clear impact of age, gender, and health on network size.
elements.sent-2411-137: The resulting models for 2024 can be found in Table~\ref{tab:regression with the referral network in Table~\ref{tab:zipref2024 -- the major implication of this model is that women are better at recruiting people than men with large and significant parameter estimates.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: In this paper, we review the core network statistics available from the King County PIT RDS survey from 2022 to 2024, possibly representing the largest network study on people experiencing homelessness in the US.
elements.sent-2411-139: We report a decrease in density (stable mean degree) between 2023 and 2024, suggesting that people experiencing homelessness have become less connected (within the community of people experiencing homelessness) over the last year.
elements.sent-2411-140: This is further emphasized by the decrease in reported acquaintances from 2022 to 2024 (halving yearly).
elements.sent-2411-141: Further, the average size of families (kinship networks) has increased (almost to the size of the reported mean degree of close friends), suggesting family units are becoming more insular over time.
elements.sent-2411-142: Further, we find that the mean degree we find from Close Friendship is 2.6 (2023) and 2.5 (2024), which closely matches important matters from the General Social Survey (GSS) and political matters from the American National Election Survey (ANES), which range from 2.14 to 2.94 \citep{lee2017important -- we interpret this as evidence we are capturing a meaningful and close social relationship to that of who people discuss important matters or politics with.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The close friendship network has a mean size of around 2.5 people in 2023 and 2024.
elements.sent-2411-144: This result of reporting around 2.5 individuals in their close network is similar to other network questions centered around close ties, such as discussing important matters in the US housed population \citep{lee2017important , emphasizing we are capturing the local support of unhoused to unhoused networks.}
elements.sent-2411-145: These ties are known to be very important for survival \cite{cummings2022social , access to resources \citep{adams2023nutritional , and well-being \citep{addo2022social .}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: Further, we see within these networks from the ZINB model of network size that gender plays an important role (women have larger networks).
elements.sent-2411-147: Last, we note that the stability of the mean degree of close friendships means that the density of the social network between people experiencing homelessness must decline as the population grows -- suggesting that the population is becoming more anomic over time.
elements.sent-2411-148: This might be because of increased displacement events or due to the increase in people newly experiencing homelessness -- both of these mechanisms are likely playing out to weaken the social support networks of the unhoused population.

elements.sent-2411-169: Here, we have carefully described the personal networks of people experiencing homelessness in King County, WA.
elements.sent-2411-170: Over the three years, we have surveyed more than 3,000 unhoused people, making this the most extensive network study of people experiencing homelessness in the United States.
elements.sent-2411-171: We have found the population of people experiencing homelessness in King County, WA, appears to be becoming less connected, less visible to their community, and more reliant on their local family structures, which are growing and heterogenous in nature (i.e., include many non-kin relationships).

elements.section-2411-010: ZIP model for peer referral network, 2024
elements.sent-2411-189: Exponential-family Random Graph Models (ERGMs) are a class of statistical models used to represent and analyze social relations within networks.
elements.sent-2411-190: They provide a flexible framework for modeling the complex dependencies in network data (\citeappendix{robins2007introduction ), and have become increasingly common through statistical packages such as STATNET (\citeappendix{handcock2008statnet ).}}
elements.sent-2411-191: ERGMs have a long history in social network analysis, from the p* models (\citeappendix{pattison1999logit ) to more recent development (\citeappendix{hunter2012computational ).}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Unlike traditional models that assume independent observations, ERGMs accommodate the interdependence of network ties by specifying the probability distribution over the set of all possible graphs (\citeappendix{robins2007introduction ).}
elements.sent-2411-193: ERG models can be written as the probability distribution over a set of graphs $G$ as follows: \begin{equation}P(G = g \mid \theta) = \frac{\exp\left(\theta^{T}s(g)\right)}{Z(\theta)},\end{equation} where $g$ is the observed graph, $\theta$ is a vector of parameters, $s(g)$ is a vector of sufficient statistics that capture graph features such as edges, triangles, and degree distributions, and $Z(\theta)$ is a normalizing constant (\citeappendix{hunter2012computational ).}
elements.sent-2411-194: Given this formulation, the key becomes estimating sufficient statistics from the data.
elements.sent-2411-195: The main challenge in estimating ERGMs arises from the normalizing constant $Z(\theta)$, which is computationally expensive to calculate because it involves summing over all possible graphs.
elements.sent-2411-196: Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods are typically employed to approximate the likelihood function and estimate model parameters (\citeappendix{hunter2012computational ).}
elements.sent-2411-197: Alternative methods, such as Maximum Pseudo-Likelihood Estimation (MPLE), are also used for faster computation.
elements.sent-2411-198: However, they may be less accurate than full MCMC-based inference (\citeappendix{hunter2012computational ) and have generally fallen out of favor as MCMC methods have become more readily available.}
elements.subsection-2411-009: Estimation of network models from sampled data
elements.sent-2411-199: In the case where we do not have complete network data, one advantage of ERGMs is that they are experiential-family models and, therefore, can be estimated directly from population statistics estimated from sampling design.
elements.sent-2411-200: This can be readily done in \citeappendix{krivitsky2017inference by specifying the target statistics.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Here, we will compute the target statistics from the network descriptive statistics in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-202: See also, \citeappendix{jenness2017epimodel for a clear description of how to compute the target statistics to fit ERGMs from population-level statistics.}
